mentions.note: Caller Chris from NY presents high-risk infrastructure play down significantly; positioned as potential next Nebius (speculative turnaround story). 'Something good could happen.' Allowed as small-position speculation.

Largest position in travel trust. Own long-term, don't trade. Brand strength allows pass-through of higher memory costs to carriers. Benefits from Alphabet AI spending. Excellent company making fortunes.

Aerospace/defense play down 60%+ from highs. Down so much that small position risk is limited (if owns at $5 goes to $4 hurts less). Allowed as speculative holding. Risk a little money on potential turnaround.

Crashed from $638 to $227 (2.5 years). Under attack from Canva/Figma (low-end design) and Claude/ChatGPT (high-end productivity). Hunting both CEO and CFO (Narayen retiring, CFO departed June 15)—massive transition risk. Hard to invest in leadership vacuum.

Defense tech stock (caller Randy). Sector under pressure: investors turned against defense due to budget concerns. Iran geopolitical spending likely to continue weighing on sector. Cramer disagrees with thesis but acknowledges headwinds.

Best-in-class credit card: smaller card base but cardholders spend more; charges fees for access to rewards. Flying since early June on 200-day MA breakout. MACD bullish. Strong while market weak. Friday earnings likely lights-out but stock historically sells off then rallies between quarters; 10:30-11am good entry.
CEO joined OpenAI and OpenAI Foundation board (Bank tech meets AI tech). Trading at low valuations vs. software. AI integration play with institutional infrastructure.

Championed by Cramer since 2008; now down significantly. Well-positioned for AgentForce AI and customer adoption but market indifferent to growth story. $50B buyback insufficient to overcome hardware-favoring narrative. Good company, not good enough for Street without higher growth.
Only real drugstore chain in America; owns Aetna health insurance. Win-win business model: drugstore revenues + insurance margins. Defensive consumer play independent of economic cycles.

CEO Raj Subramaniam ('Superman') executing excellently on consolidating express to ground operations. Own into holiday season. Kept in travel trust (usually trims one of pair). Strong execution on integration strategy.
FedEx Freight spin-off terrific; can take out costs and be much more focused than consolidated structure. Kept in travel trust alongside FDX (rare dual holding). Structural advantage from separation.
Bank tech company trading at valuations radically lower than software. Tech embedded in banking operations. Good value diversification candidate away from memory stock concentration.

Tax software down 56%. Customers switching to free AI chatbots (ChatGPT) for tax prep instead of TurboTax. Structural disruption: generative AI commoditizes tax software. Business model under existential threat.

Pharma innovator with AAA balance sheet. Invents life-saving drugs practically every month. Sold band-aid business; now pure-play healthcare innovation. Defensive, quality diversification away from concentration risk.
Regional bank preferred to Truist. Ohio operations a plus ('great state to do business'). Similar profile to TFC but better execution and regional strength justify preference.

Acquires Kenvue (J&J consumer health) for $48.7B; $2B synergies, defensive consolidation. Dividend king (4.7% yield, 54 consecutive increases). Protected from foreign competition. Long-term opportunity; expect Q2 miss creates entry.

Tech company in bank clothing; bullish trend channel (higher highs/lows). MACD buy signal last month. RSI trending higher but not overbought. High volume. Hasn't taken January highs yet ($35 resistance at $573). Reports next week with potential capital return catalyst.

Magnificent Q2: organic growth 5.4% (best 5+ years), EPS +11%, cash conversion 107%, guidance raised. Innovation restarted (350+ new products, vitality index climbing). Data center Microsoft deal (expanded beam optics, 100 patents). Industrial safety growth. PFAS resolved. CEO execution excellent.

Memory chip maker preferred to Nvidia on national security grounds. Pulled back from 1,200 high to 970; good value after pullback. Best option for memory exposure given Micron trade secret case precedent.

Strong CEO Bill McDermott integrating AI excellently but 'nothing's ever enough.' Last quarter was upside surprise that 'meant very little'—possibly meaningless. Market has no use for enterprise software even with good execution.
Best-run company in world; own long-term, don't trade. Largest position in travel trust. Clients praise and work against it; China exposure controversial but national security concerns legitimate.

Discount retailer missing quarters consistently; weak execution. Caller Will bought at 94, 88, now 70; stock broke down. TJX owns the segment and executes better; stick with TJX instead (though even TJX struggling).
New CEO Ricky Lores (from HP). Stripe interested as suitor; potential acquisition play. 10x earnings. Good risk-reward but not great. Cramer prefers traditional credit card companies over BNPL model.
Super-regional bank with strong recent earnings, low PE, 4% dividend yield. Cramer prefers KeyCorp (KEY) instead due to regional operations and execution track record. Passed over in lightning round.

MACD bullish crossover mid-June; on-balance volume spiking (institutional buying climax). Textbook uptrend. $20B buyback likely to expand. Technical chart suggests $400 by fall. No credit risk (unlike AmEx). Gloved by mutual funds.

Turmoil at top with co-founder Neil Bushry returning as CEO but stock unmoved. Return to founding leadership doesn't matter in hardware-only market. Enterprise software growth no longer impressive enough to overcome sector rotation.
Bank tech company trading at valuations radically lower than software. Tech embedded in banking operations. Good value diversification candidate away from memory stock concentration.
Website builder down 50% from highs. Under attack from AI (Claude, ChatGPT) and site builders (Sleeper-in-it). Losing share to generative AI disruption. Structural obsolescence risk.
